key_points: Triples are cramped with shared closets; common for juniors
Resident parking waitlist closes early, causing major headaches
Townhouses have only 2 washers/dryers shared among ~40 people
key_quotes: The housing is difficult and my son has been in a triple for the past two years. Extremely tight and sharing closets.
Housing is wack but just rent nearby.
Only 2 washers and 2 dryers for each townhouse. So like 40 people are using the same ones.

key_points: ~70% commuter population makes campus feel dead, especially weekends
Dorm residents find active social life; commuters struggle to connect
Gaming club, META club, and esports teams exist for introverts
key_quotes: Honestly the weekends are pretty dead here too unless you have friends who also dorm.
I agree it's definitely more of a commuter school, I heard 70% of the students are commuters.
I dorm and would say that I know 1/10 by name when in the dining hall.

key_points: Marillac dining hall is functional but unremarkable
Law school food court praised as notably better than undergrad options
No strong positive feedback on dining quality from current students
key_quotes: Former johnny. I would like to say that Law school has the best food court ever.
There was plenty of space in the dining hall in Marillac.

key_points: No direct praise or mention of counseling services in student posts
PharmD program's ~30% retention rate causes significant student anxiety
Accessibility office mentioned as responsive for physical disabilities
key_quotes: I read through some of these posts and saw that there's like a 30% retention rate and I'm afraid of the consequences of my choice.

I recently broke my ankle bones and have to stay home for most of this semester, contacted student accessibility, and according to the director at least, he's already had multiple students coming to him.

key_points: Generous merit scholarships available but mostly front-loaded; aid shrinks each year
Full $34k merit scholarship attainable around 4.4 GPA / 1400 SAT
Without scholarships, sticker price is brutal—especially for low-income students
key_quotes: I personally got a scholarship that did cover a large amount of the tuition, but even then it's still several thousands a semester (12k-20k a year, getting higher with each year).
Even after all the aid and scholarship, I still have to pay around 12k with single parent.
I got a full 34k + 3k for doing the SJU College Advantage Program.

key_points: PharmD program is rigorous with poor professor quality flagged repeatedly
Pre-med track has solid outcomes; multiple students report med school acceptance
Some professors ignore emails and lack posted office hours—no accountability
key_quotes: 8/10 profs are terrible. You can try to suck up to them but I just couldn't.
My professor for microeconomics told me to hand in an assignment in-person on Tuesday. He has not answered emails in the past, and has no office hours listed.
Went to St Johns. Practicing medicine x 10 years. My experience there was some of the best times of my life.

key_points: Financial aid office requires students to chase them down for corrections
Transfer credit policy is restrictive—can't take elsewhere what SJU offers
Graduation ticket allocation causes annual scramble; limited to 5 per student
key_quotes: You'll get it in addition to your scholarship, but I believe it should be included already, if not then inform the financial aid office and they can fix it.
You have to ask your advisor. But for me say you want to take chemistry during the summer they won't allow you to take it somewhere else if they have it at St. John's.
Interesting how this stuff hasn't changed... where is the money that the school gets? Why aren't there more support for commuter students?

key_points: Jamaica, Queens commute exposes students to harassment and safety concerns
Campus itself is clean but library is small and underwhelming
NYC access is a major draw but campus feels isolated from it
key_quotes: Commuting through Jamaica, you sometimes get harassed and met by rude and violent people.
The library sucks ngl - people are always talking in there... overall underwhelming.
It's a basic library nothing special. Other colleges have much better and more beautiful libraries.

key_points: Career development office praised for job placements, especially in law
One alum warns of struggles finding employment after graduation
NYC location creates real internship pipeline, especially for business and legal
key_quotes: I went to SJU Law I liked it a lot especially career development office helping with job placements.
I graduated with years worth of loan debt to pay off and struggled to find employment. I actually wish I could go back and tell myself not to go.
I'm a junior right now, and I'm on the pre-law track and I love St. John's! I've had so many opportunities to have legal internships.

key_points: Without heavy scholarship, near-universal advice is: don't do it
Alumni directly warn against taking on $100k+ debt for this degree
Best value only if merit aid brings cost below $10k/year
key_quotes: Absolutely fucking not. [in response to 'Is St. John's worth 32k a year?']
It's not a good idea to be a young person with $100k+ in student loans, don't talk yourself into that at any university.
I only went because I got scholarships that put my tuition below 10k a year. Even then I was considering just going to my local community college.

key_points: Basketball hype is real and creates rare moments of campus unity
School feels like a commuter school even to its own students
Strong sense of NYC identity but campus culture itself is thin
key_quotes: We're way too big of a school for our sub to be this dead. We're not Fordham academically but I feel like we're more quintessentially NYC.
We live on Long Island but my son is a resident. He loves it there. There's plenty to do there when you live there.
Should be all caps so people go to Trads or Parsons on some hype shit.

key_points: **Debt Risk:** Alumni explicitly warn $32k/year isn't worth it without heavy aid
**PharmD Retention:** ~30% of pharmacy students don't make it through the program
**Safety:** Commuters report harassment and violence in the Jamaica transit corridor
key_quotes: I graduated with years worth of loan debt to pay off and struggled to find employment. No degree is worth the thousands St. John's is asking.
I read through some of these posts and saw that there's like a 30% retention rate and I'm afraid of the consequences of my choice.
Commuting through Jamaica, you sometimes get harassed and met by rude and violent people.

key_points: BS/MD combined program with Grenada hospital partnership is a rare pre-med fast track
Student tickets to MSG basketball games are only $10—legitimate NYC experience
Tech Commons has free bookable PCs and active esports teams most students don't know about
key_quotes: I've been accepted into St. John's BS/MD program, in partnership with their hospital in Grenada!
You can get them from Carnesecca close to game day, or at MSG day of. They're $10 for students.
Come by the Tech Commons M-F 9-5! We have PCs you can book on the Connect app for free and lots of various esports teams.
